challenges.explanation: print() outputs the text inside the parentheses exactly as written, including the comma and exclamation mark. The surrounding double quotes are not printed — they just tell Python that it is a piece of text.

challenges.prompt: This code has a bug. What is wrong?
options: The message needs single quotes instead of double quotes
Print should be lowercase — Python is case-sensitive, so it must be print
There should be a semicolon at the end of the line
challenges.explanation: Python is case-sensitive. The built-in function is called print (all lowercase). Using Print or PRINT causes a NameError because Python does not recognise them as the same thing.

challenges.prompt: Complete the code so it prints your name. Fill in the function name that displays text on screen.
challenges.explanation: print() is Python's built-in function for displaying output. Whatever you put inside the parentheses (wrapped in quotes for text) gets shown on screen.

challenges.prompt: Put these lines in the right order so the code prints both facts about Python.
lines: print(language + " was first released in", year)
challenges.explanation: You must define variables before you can use them. language and year are stored first, then print() uses both of them to build the output message.
